planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or seedlings in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade; wasabi requires cool, constantly moving, mineral-rich water (streams or regular irrigation with mineral-rich water) and temperatures between 46–68°F — hot summers above 80°F will kill or severely stress it. In hotter climates deep shade, consistent watering, and a cool microclimate are essential. The edible part is the thick rhizome, which takes 18–24 months to reach usable size.

directions: Plant in constantly moist, cool, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade; wasabi is among the most challenging and most coveted of culinary herbs — the genuine pungent root condiment used in Japanese cuisine is irreplaceable by the horseradish-and-green-dye substitute sold widely as 'wasabi paste.' It grows naturally along cool, shaded mountain stream banks in Japan, where it is cultivated in channels of continuously flowing, mineral-rich spring water under dense forest canopy. The combination of strict cool-temperature requirements (above 68°F it declines, above 80°F it may die), full shade, and consistently high, reliable moisture makes it one of the most exacting herbs to grow in most American gardens. It produces handsome, heart-shaped, glossy leaves on long petioles that are attractive in a shaded, moist position, and after 18–24 months the thick, knobby, pale-green rhizome is ready to harvest and freshly grate — releasing the volatile, sinus-clearing, horseradish-like compounds far more nuanced and complex than anything in a tube. In zones 8–10 it can succeed in deeply shaded, constantly moist, cool microclimates such as the north-facing side of a house or under dense tree canopy near water.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall (require cold stratification) in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; osha is native to moist mountain meadows and open forests of the Rocky Mountains at 6,000–11,000 feet elevation and prefers cool summers — it may struggle in hot, humid lowland climates. The deep, dark-brown, hairy roots with a distinctive celery-like fragrance are the part used medicinally.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun (in cool climates); osha is one of the most important medicinal plants of the Southwest and Rocky Mountain Native American healing traditions — the pungent, dark-brown, shaggy-coated roots with their distinctive, strong, celery-fennel fragrance were used for centuries by Pueblo, Navajo, and other western peoples for respiratory conditions, immune support, and as bear medicine (bears seek out and roll in the roots emerging from hibernation, and the plant is sometimes called bear root). The large, finely divided, carrot-family foliage is attractive in the garden, and in summer the plants produce typical flat-topped umbel flowers in white. It grows naturally in cool, moist, mountain meadows of the Rocky Mountains from New Mexico to Wyoming at high elevations where cool summer temperatures persist. In lowland gardens it requires the coolest, most protected, shadiest positions available and adequate moisture. A plant of great ethnobotanical significance and unique aromatic character.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in spring (do not cover — needs light to germinate) in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; weld is biennial to short-lived perennial, forming a rosette in the first year and flowering in the second. It self-sows freely in lean, disturbed soils. The entire above-ground plant is harvested for dye at full flower for the deepest, most lasting yellow.

directions: Surface sow seeds in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; weld is a Mediterranean native biennial that was, for two millennia before the development of synthetic dyes, the primary source of the most important yellow dye in European textile history — yielding from the whole plant at flower a brilliant, pure, fast, golden-yellow color from the flavonoid luteolin (the plant's specific epithet) that was far more color-fast than the yellow of many competing dye plants and combined with blue woad or indigo to produce the green used in many historical tapestries and textiles. Archaeological evidence of weld cultivation dates to Neolithic Europe. As a garden plant it is ornamental in a quiet way — producing tall, narrow, spike-like racemes of small, four-petaled, pale-yellow-green flowers in summer. The self-sowing habit maintains naturalized colonies in disturbed, lean, sunny situations. A meaningful plant for natural dyers, herb gardens, and historical plant collections, and an important species for the sustainable textile and craft revival of plant-based natural dyeing.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds where plants are to grow permanently in spring (or fall in mild climates) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Indian dill does not transplant well due to its taproot. It can be sown more thickly than European dill and the young plants thinned for fresh leaf use while remaining plants mature for seed harvest. The seeds are larger than common dill and more pungently aromatic.

directions: Direct sow seeds in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Indian dill is a distinct species from the common European dill (Anethum graveolens), native to South Asia and widely used throughout Indian, Sri Lankan, and Iranian cuisines where it occupies a culinary role analogous to but distinct from European dill — the seeds are larger, more flattened, with a stronger, earthier, more camphoraceous aroma than common dill, and the fresh leaves have a more pungent, slightly different flavor profile that is essential to certain South Asian fish curries, lentil dishes, and the Persian herb-rice dishes that are among the oldest continuously prepared foods in culinary history. The whole plant is taller and somewhat coarser than common dill. The seeds are used both whole in tempering (bloom in hot oil to release aromatics) and ground in spice blends. The essential oil chemistry differs meaningfully from A. graveolens, containing higher carvone concentrations that give the seeds their more assertive character. It is used medicinally throughout South Asia as a carminative and digestive herb. Excellent for South Asian herb gardens and cross-cultural culinary herb collections.

planting.note: Root stem or leaf cuttings in warm, moist medium or in water; baby rubber plant is very easy to propagate and is one of the most forgiving houseplants for indoor culture. It tolerates occasional underwatering (its thick leaves store moisture) and low light, but resents overwatering — allow the soil to dry slightly between waterings. In zones 10–12 it grows outdoors as a shade-loving groundcover or border plant.

directions: Grow in bright, indirect light indoors or in filtered shade outdoors in zones 10–12; baby rubber plant is one of the most reliably successful and forgiving houseplants in cultivation — a Central and South American tropical native producing thick, rounded, deeply glossy, dark-green leaves on compact, upright to spreading stems that create a neat, controlled, lush mound of tropical foliage in indoor environments. The fleshy, somewhat succulent leaves store moisture and allow the plant to tolerate periods of drought far better than most non-succulent houseplants, making it an excellent choice for occasional-waterers and forgetful gardeners. It thrives in the low-to-medium indirect light typical of indoor environments and in the higher humidity of kitchens and bathrooms. Variegated cultivars are widely available, including 'Variegata' (cream and green marbled leaves), making the genus among the most popular of all houseplant groups. In tropical zones it grows as a semi-succulent shade groundcover. Easy to propagate from stem cuttings placed directly in moist potting mix. It is closely related to the ornamental pepper family (Piperaceae) and its genus contains over 1,000 species.
